message.content:
You are an adversarial refuter. ASSUME this finding is FALSE and try to prove it. Read the actual code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay and find the code path / contract / test that makes the PoC impossible or the concern moot. Only conclude survives=true if you genuinely cannot disprove it.

Finding C-1: lock_timeout session-GUC leaks into pooled connection used for all later checkpoint writes
File: src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py:212
Description: During schema bootstrap _acquire_migration_lock runs `SELECT set_config('lock_timeout', %s, false)` with is_local=false, i.e. SESSION scope. The connection is drawn from `pool` via `with pool.connection() as conn:` and, on block exit, is RETURNED to that same pool. psycopg_pool does NOT reset session state on check-in unless a `reset=` callback is configured on ConnectionPool (none is). The very same pool is then handed to PostgresSaver(pool) and used for every subsequent checkpoint read/write. So the bootstrap-only lock_timeout (default 30s) silently persists as a session setting on a long-lived pooled connection and applies to ordinary checkpoint operations. Under lock contention a normal checkpoint write that would otherwise block briefly will instead abort with 'canceling statement due to lock_timeout', i.e. durability loss at exactly the interrupt/resume the checkpointer exists to guarantee. is_local=true was avoided because under autocommit a transaction-local SET would not survive to the separate pg_advisory_lock statement — so the fix is to explicitly RESET after acquiring, or run bootstrap on a connection that is closed (not returned to the pool).
PoC: 1) ASSAY_MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS=30000 (default). 2) get_checkpointer() bootstraps; the bootstrap connection has lock_timeout='30s' set at session scope and is returned to the pool. 3) Two runs resume concurrently and contend on the same checkpoint row; a checkpoint UPDATE waits on the row lock >30s and raises 'canceling statement due to lock_timeout' instead of completing — a spurious durability failure. Reproduce by asserting `conn.execute("SHOW lock_timeout").fetchone()` is not '0' on a connection re-checked-out from the pool after bootstrap.

Return survives=false if you can refute it (with the specific reason), survives=true if it withstands a genuine disproof attempt.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
1	"""Durable run-state checkpointer — local, data-sovereign (ADR-0003).
2	
3	The orchestration graph persists its full state (current phase, gate decisions, locked
4	hypotheses, verdicts) through a checkpointer so a run can interrupt at a human-approval gate
5	and resume later. That state holds the entire analysis, so the store is **on-box only**
6	(ADR-0003); a SaaS checkpointer is disqualified.
7	
8	This module is the LangGraph checkpointer factory, ported faithfully from the prior
9	platform's hardened implementation and generalized (dataset-agnostic ``ASSAY_*`` env, no
10	config-file coupling — see ADR-0006). The hardened behaviors are deliberately preserved
11	because each prevents a real production failure:
12	
13	- a **self-healing ConnectionPool** (not a raw connection): when Postgres goes away — node
14	  sleep, restart, idle timeout, a network blip — a cached raw connection is permanently
15	  broken and every later checkpoint op raises, i.e. durability is lost exactly during the
16	  interrupt/resume the checkpointer exists to support. The pool runs a liveness check on
17	  checkout and replaces dead connections;
18	- a **session advisory lock** serializing first-time schema setup across *processes*, run on
19	  the *same* connection as the DDL (a pool would otherwise check out a different connection,
20	  leaving the lock ineffective);
21	- a **once-per-process-per-conn guard** (``setup()`` is idempotent but re-locking + replaying
22	  the migration query on every call is wasteful), with an in-process lock making the
23	  check-and-set atomic so concurrent threads don't both run setup;
24	- **credential redaction**: a backend exception (psycopg/psycopg_pool) can embed the full
25	  ``user:password`` URI/DSN in its own message, so the conn string is scrubbed precisely and
26	  the exception text via URI-userinfo + DSN-password backstops; the redacted message is then
27	  raised *outside* the exception handler, so the credential-bearing original is retained as
28	  neither ``__cause__`` nor ``__context__`` (a logger walking ``__context__`` cannot
29	  re-expose it);
30	- a single shared **atexit** pool-cleanup handler (one per process, not one per call) so the
31	  pool's worker thread is joined before interpreter finalization.
32	
33	LangGraph/psycopg are optional (the ``persistence`` extra) and imported lazily; the
34	credential-redaction and connection-resolution logic is pure and unit-tested offline.
35	"""
36	
37	from __future__ import annotations
38	
39	import atexit
40	import os
41	import re
42	import threading
43	from typing import Any, cast
44	from urllib.parse import urlsplit
45	
46	from assay_engine._local import require_local_uri
47	
48	_DEFAULT_PG = "postgresql://localhost:5432/assay"
49	
50	# Arbitrary fixed key for the Postgres session advisory lock that serializes checkpoint
51	# schema migrations across concurrent initializers. The value is irrelevant beyond being a
52	# stable per-logical-schema constant; "ASSY" as an int.
53	_MIGRATION_LOCK_KEY = 0x4153_5359
54	
55	# Upper bound (milliseconds) on how long the cross-process advisory lock may be waited for
56	# during schema bootstrap. A stuck/crashed peer that still holds a live PG session on the lock
57	# must NOT be able to hang bootstrap forever (#129); lock_timeout aborts the wait. 0 disables
58	# the bound (block indefinitely, the pre-#129 behavior). Read fresh on each bootstrap (#F-033) so
59	# an env var set after import still takes effect; the module constant is the default + the value
60	# tests monkeypatch.
61	_MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS = max(0, int(os.environ.get("ASSAY_MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS", "30000")))
62	
63	
64	def _migration_lock_timeout_ms() -> int:
65	    """The advisory-lock wait bound, re-read from the env each call (#F-033).
66	
67	    Falls back to the import-time module constant when the env var is unset, so a test that
68	    monkeypatches ``_MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS`` still drives the value.
69	    """
70	    raw = os.environ.get("ASSAY_MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS")
71	    if raw is None:
72	        return _MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS
73	    return max(0, int(raw))
74	
75	
76	# Brief meta-lock guarding the process-local registries below (atexit registration, the open-pool
77	# list, the per-conn lock map, the pool cache). It is held ONLY for in-memory check-and-set — it
78	# is NEVER held across blocking cross-process I/O (pool.connection(), the advisory-lock wait, or
79	# setup() DDL). That blocking work is serialized per conn_str instead (#129), so a stuck advisory
80	# lock on one database cannot block initialization of an unrelated one.
81	_init_lock = threading.Lock()
82	
83	# Per-conn_str bootstrap locks: only same-conn_str initializers serialize; different databases
84	# bootstrap concurrently. Created lazily under _init_lock.
85	_CONN_INIT_LOCKS: dict[str, threading.Lock] = {}
86	
87	# Live pools cached by conn_str so the factory reuses one self-healing pool (and its worker
88	# thread) per database rather than opening a fresh pool on every call (#144).
89	_POOLS_BY_CONN: dict[str, Any] = {}
90	
91	# Pools opened by the factory, closed once at process exit via a single shared atexit handler
92	# (registering one handler per call would accumulate handlers across repeated factory calls).
93	_OPEN_POOLS: list[Any] = []
94	_atexit_registered = False
95	
96	# Strips ``://user:password@`` (URI userinfo) from any URI-like substring. '/' is allowed
97	# inside the userinfo so a password containing '/' is still redacted; only '@' (the userinfo
98	# terminator) and whitespace bound the match.
99	_CREDS_RE = re.compile(r"://[^@\s]+@")
100	# Strips a libpq keyword/value DSN password token (``password=...`` / ``pgpassword=...``),
101	# handling quoted values that may contain spaces.
102	_DSN_PW_RE = re.compile(r"(?i)\b(password|pgpassword)\s*=\s*('[^']*'|\"[^\"]*\"|\S+)")
103	
104	
105	def _sanitize_conn_str(conn_str: str) -> str:
106	    """Return ``scheme://host[:port]/path`` with any ``user:password`` userinfo stripped.
107	
108	    Robust to malformed userinfo: a password containing ``/``/``#`` makes ``urlsplit``'s
109	    ``.port`` raise ``ValueError`` on access, so the whole parse (including attribute access)
110	    is guarded and falls back to the generic userinfo-strip regex / a constant placeholder.
111	    """
112	    try:
113	        u = urlsplit(conn_str)
114	        host = u.hostname or ""
115	        port = u.port
116	        scheme = u.scheme
117	        path = u.path
118	    except ValueError:
119	        return _CREDS_RE.sub("://", conn_str)
120	    if not host:
121	        return _CREDS_RE.sub("://", conn_str) if scheme else "<store>"
122	    if ":" in host:  # IPv6 literal — urlsplit drops the brackets; restore them
123	        host = f"[{host}]"
124	    netloc = f"{host}:{port}" if port else host
125	    return f"{scheme}://{netloc}{path}" if scheme else "<store>"
126	
127	
128	def redact_creds(text: str, conn_str: str = "") -> str:
129	    """Remove credentials a backend exception may embed in its message text.
130	
131	    Layers, in order of precision:
132	
133	    1. exact replacement of the known ``conn_str`` (handles any password characters, incl.
134	       ``/`` and spaces) — this is the precise layer and covers the real connection string;
135	    2. a generic URI-userinfo strip (``://user:pass@`` → ``://``) as a backstop for any
136	       *other* URI in the text;
137	    3. a libpq DSN ``password=``/``pgpassword=`` strip for the keyword/value form.
138	
139	    Layers 2–3 are best-effort for *secondary* endpoints (e.g. a replica DSN) the caller did
140	    not pass as ``conn_str``; the primary connection string is always scrubbed precisely.
141	    """
142	    if conn_str:
143	        text = text.replace(conn_str, _sanitize_conn_str(conn_str))
144	    text = _CREDS_RE.sub("://", text)
145	    return _DSN_PW_RE.sub(r"\1=***", text)
146	
147	
148	def get_postgres_connection_string() -> str:
149	    """Resolve the Postgres connection string (``ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL`` env → loopback default).
150	
151	    The env path bypasses any external config validation, so loopback is enforced here at the
152	    point of use (ADR-0003 defense-in-depth): graph state must not leave the box.
153	    """
154	    url = os.environ.get("ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL") or _DEFAULT_PG
155	    return require_local_uri(url, what="checkpointer connection")
156	
157	
158	def _safe_close(pool: Any) -> None:
159	    """Close one pool, swallowing errors so a single failing ``close()`` does not abort
160	    cleanup of the remaining pools at interpreter exit (``close()`` calls a C ``finish()``
161	    that can raise)."""
162	    try:
163	        pool.close()
164	    except Exception:
165	        pass
166	
167	
168	def _close_all_pools() -> None:
169	    # Snapshot under the meta-lock before iterating (#F-020): _OPEN_POOLS is appended under
170	    # _init_lock elsewhere, and on a free-threaded (GIL-less) build a concurrent list.append
171	    # reallocation during iteration is undefined behavior. _safe_close never takes _init_lock, so
172	    # closing outside the lock cannot deadlock.
173	    with _init_lock:
174	        pools = list(_OPEN_POOLS)
175	    for p in pools:
176	        _safe_close(p)
177	
178	
179	def _register_pool_cleanup(pool: Any) -> None:
180	    global _atexit_registered
181	    with _init_lock:
182	        _OPEN_POOLS.append(pool)
183	        if not _atexit_registered:
184	            atexit.register(_close_all_pools)
185	            _atexit_registered = True
186	
187	
188	def _conn_init_lock(conn_str: str) -> threading.Lock:
189	    """Return the per-conn_str bootstrap lock, creating it under the brief meta-lock (#129)."""
190	    with _init_lock:
191	        lock = _CONN_INIT_LOCKS.get(conn_str)
192	        if lock is None:
193	            lock = _CONN_INIT_LOCKS[conn_str] = threading.Lock()
194	        return lock
195	
196	
197	def _cached_pool(conn_str: str) -> Any:
198	    with _init_lock:
199	        return _POOLS_BY_CONN.get(conn_str)
200	
201	
202	def _acquire_migration_lock(conn: Any) -> None:
203	    """Acquire the cross-process schema-migration advisory lock with a bounded wait (#129).
204	
205	    ``lock_timeout`` aborts the ``pg_advisory_lock`` wait if a peer holds it past the deadline,
206	    so a stuck/crashed initializer cannot hang bootstrap indefinitely; the resulting error
207	    propagates as a redacted ``RuntimeError`` like any other backend failure. set_config is used
208	    (not ``SET``) so the value can be safely parameterized.
209	    """
210	    timeout_ms = _migration_lock_timeout_ms()
211	    if timeout_ms:
212	        conn.execute("SELECT set_config('lock_timeout', %s, false)", (str(timeout_ms),))
213	    conn.execute("SELECT pg_advisory_lock(%s)", (_MIGRATION_LOCK_KEY,))
214	
215	
216	def get_checkpointer(use_memory: bool = False) -> Any:
217	    """Return a configured LangGraph checkpointer.
218	
219	    ``use_memory=True`` returns an in-memory saver (tests / no-Postgres). Otherwise a
220	    Postgres-backed saver over a self-healing pool, with advisory-locked one-time schema
221	    setup. Raises ``RuntimeError`` (with credentials redacted) if the backend is unreachable
222	    or the extra is not installed.
223	    """
224	    try:
225	        if use_memory:
226	            from langgraph.checkpoint.memory import MemorySaver
227	
228	            return MemorySaver()
229	        from langgraph.checkpoint.postgres import PostgresSaver
230	        from psycopg.rows import dict_row
231	        from psycopg_pool import ConnectionPool
232	    except ImportError as exc:  # pragma: no cover - exercised only without the extra
233	        raise RuntimeError(
234	            "Postgres checkpointer requires the 'persistence' extra "
235	            "(langgraph-checkpoint-postgres, psycopg[binary,pool]) — not installed"
236	        ) from exc
237	
238	    conn_str = get_postgres_connection_string()
239	
240	    # Fast path: reuse a live, self-healing pool for this conn_str rather than opening a new pool
241	    # (and its background worker thread) on every call (#144). The liveness check on checkout
242	    # replaces dead connections, so a cached pool stays valid across transient outages.
243	    cached = _cached_pool(conn_str)
244	    if cached is not None:
245	        return PostgresSaver(cast(Any, cached))
246	
247	    # Serialize bootstrap PER conn_str — NOT process-globally (#129). Unrelated databases
248	    # initialize concurrently, and a stuck advisory lock on one conn_str can never block init of
249	    # another. The brief meta-lock (_init_lock) is only taken inside the helpers, never held
250	    # across the blocking advisory-lock wait / DDL below.
251	    with _conn_init_lock(conn_str):
252	        cached = _cached_pool(conn_str)  # another thread may have built it while we waited
253	        if cached is not None:
254	            return PostgresSaver(cast(Any, cached))
255	
256	        failure: str | None = None
257	        pool: Any = None
258	        try:
259	            # Pool bounds are env-tunable (#F-040): the reasoning seam already runs 8 worker
260	            # threads, so a fixed max_size=8 can be saturated under concurrent reasoning+checkpoint
261	            # load on larger hardware. connect_timeout bounds an individual TCP connect so a
262	            # black-holed host fails fast instead of hanging bootstrap for the OS default.
263	            min_size = max(0, int(os.environ.get("ASSAY_POOL_MIN_SIZE", "1")))
264	            max_size = max(min_size or 1, int(os.environ.get("ASSAY_POOL_MAX_SIZE", "8")))
265	            connect_timeout = max(1, int(os.environ.get("ASSAY_POOL_CONNECT_TIMEOUT", "10")))
266	            pool = ConnectionPool(
267	                conn_str,
268	                min_size=min_size,
269	                max_size=max_size,
270	                kwargs={
271	                    "autocommit": True,
272	                    "prepare_threshold": 0,
273	                    "row_factory": dict_row,
274	                    "connect_timeout": connect_timeout,
275	                },
276	                check=ConnectionPool.check_connection,  # liveness check on checkout (self-healing)
277	                open=True,
278	            )
279	            _register_pool_cleanup(pool)
280	            # Bootstrap the schema once per process per conn_str. The advisory lock and the DDL
281	            # must run on the SAME connection (pg_advisory_lock is session-scoped); a pool-backed
282	            # setup() would lock one connection and DDL on another, leaving the lock ineffective.
283	            with pool.connection() as conn:
284	                _acquire_migration_lock(conn)  # bounded wait so a stuck peer can't hang us (#129)
285	                try:
286	                    # row_factory=dict_row makes this connection dict-rowed at runtime
287	                    # (what PostgresSaver requires); the cast asserts that runtime truth
288	                    # the type system can't infer from the pool kwargs.
289	                    PostgresSaver(cast(Any, conn)).setup()  # DDL on the locked connection
290	                finally:
291	                    # Best-effort unlock; the lock auto-releases when the connection
292	                    # returns to the pool, and raising here would mask a setup() error.
293	                    try:
294	                        conn.execute("SELECT pg_advisory_unlock(%s)", (_MIGRATION_LOCK_KEY,))
295	                    except Exception:
296	                        pass
297	            with _init_lock:
298	                _POOLS_BY_CONN[conn_str] = pool  # cache for reuse on later calls (#144)
299	            return PostgresSaver(cast(Any, pool))  # dict-rowed pool (row_factory=dict_row)
300	        except Exception as exc:
301	            # Build the redacted message here, but raise it OUTSIDE the handler (below) so the
302	            # original — credential-bearing — exception is retained as neither __cause__ NOR
303	            # __context__ (a logger walking __context__ would otherwise re-expose the password).
304	            failure = (
305	                f"Postgres checkpointer connection failed (is Postgres running on "
306	                f"{_sanitize_conn_str(conn_str)}?): {redact_creds(str(exc), conn_str)}. "
307	                "For tests without Postgres, use get_checkpointer(use_memory=True)."
308	            )
309	            # The pool may have opened before bootstrap failed — close it and drop it from the
310	            # cleanup registry so a failed init does not leak the connection/worker thread (#105).
311	            if pool is not None:
312	                _safe_close(pool)
313	                with _init_lock:
314	                    if pool in _OPEN_POOLS:
315	                        _OPEN_POOLS.remove(pool)
316	        raise RuntimeError(failure)
317	
318	
319	def configured_checkpointer(use_memory: bool | None = None) -> Any:
320	    """Return the checkpointer the environment selects.
321	
322	    ``use_memory`` (or ``ASSAY_CHECKPOINT_BACKEND=memory``) chooses the in-memory saver;
323	    otherwise Postgres.
324	    """
325	    if use_memory is None:
326	        use_memory = os.environ.get("ASSAY_CHECKPOINT_BACKEND", "postgres").lower() == "memory"
327	    return get_checkpointer(use_memory=use_memory)
328

content.text:
The finding's core claim is correct about line 212 (`is_local=false` = SESSION scope) and that no `reset=` callback is set on the pool. But I need to verify the critical mechanical claim: does the bootstrap connection actually get RETURNED to the pool and reused, carrying the session GUC?

The key question is what `pg_advisory_unlock` and pool check-in do. Let me check the psycopg_pool behavior and whether there's a `reset` configured anywhere, plus check for any reset logic.

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && python -c "import psycopg_pool; print(psycopg_pool.__version__)" 2>/dev/null; pip show psycopg_pool 2>/dev/null | grep -i version

content.content:

src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py:205:    ``lock_timeout`` aborts the ``pg_advisory_lock`` wait if a peer holds it past the deadline,
src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py:207:    propagates as a redacted ``RuntimeError`` like any other backend failure. set_config is used
src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py:210:    timeout_ms = _migration_lock_timeout_ms()
src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py:212:        conn.execute("SELECT set_config('lock_timeout', %s, false)", (str(timeout_ms),))
tests/test_persistence.py:354:    offline, and reset the module's process-local bootstrap state."""
tests/test_persistence.py:364:        "lock_timeout_on": [],
tests/test_persistence.py:372:            if "lock_timeout" in sql:
tests/test_persistence.py:373:                state["lock_timeout_on"].append(params)
tests/test_persistence.py:427:    # reset process-local bootstrap bookkeeping
tests/test_persistence.py:476:    # #129: the advisory lock must be acquired under a bounded lock_timeout so a stuck peer
tests/test_persistence.py:477:    # holding it cannot hang bootstrap forever. set_config('lock_timeout', ...) runs before the
tests/test_persistence.py:481:    assert state["lock_timeout_on"], "no lock_timeout set before advisory lock (#129)"
tests/test_persistence.py:483:    (params,) = state["lock_timeout_on"]
tests/test_persistence.py:671:def test_migration_lock_timeout_is_read_fresh_from_env(fake_pg, monkeypatch):

content.text:
psycopg_pool is not installed locally, so I must reason from the library's documented contract (psycopg 3.2 pool). This is the crux of the finding.

The psycopg_pool `ConnectionPool` contract: when a connection is returned to the pool (on `with pool.connection()` exit), the pool calls `_return_connection`, which:
1. Rolls back any open transaction (`conn.rollback()` if not in autocommit / or always to clear state).
2. Runs the `reset` callback **if one is configured**.

The key documented fact: psycopg_pool does NOT issue `DISCARD ALL` or `RESET ALL` by default. The default check-in only does a `rollback()` to end any pending transaction. Session-level GUCs set via `SET` / `set_config(..., is_local=false)` are **transaction-independent** and survive a `rollback()`. So a SESSION-scoped `lock_timeout` does persist across check-in unless a `reset` callback explicitly clears it.

This confirms the finding's mechanical claim. Now let me verify the connection is genuinely reused — i.e., min_size=1 means the bootstrap connection stays in the pool. With `min_size=1`, the single connection used for bootstrap is the same one returned and reused for checkpoint operations. Let me confirm there's no other mitigating factor, like the bootstrap using a separate connection or `lock_timeout` being reset on the advisory lock path.

content.text:
Confirmed. Let me reason carefully through the one possible disproof: does the `lock_timeout` GUC actually cause spurious checkpoint write failures, and is the bootstrap connection actually reused?

Let me reconsider the PoC's impact claim critically — this is where a refutation might exist. The finding claims lock contention on a checkpoint row UPDATE would block >30s and abort. But PostgresSaver writes checkpoints. Two concurrent runs with **distinct thread_ids** write distinct checkpoint rows — no row-lock contention. The finding's PoC requires "two runs resume concurrently and contend on the same checkpoint row." Whether that's a realistic scenario matters for severity, but the core defect (the GUC leak) is real regardless of whether row contention is common.

However, my job is to refute the finding's central technical claim, not just argue severity. The central claim is:

1. `set_config(..., false)` sets SESSION-scope lock_timeout — TRUE (verified in code, line 212).
2. No `reset=` callback on the pool — TRUE (verified, lines 266-278 have no `reset=`).
3. psycopg_pool default check-in does NOT clear session GUCs — TRUE per the library contract (only rollback, no DISCARD/RESET).
4. The bootstrap connection is returned to the same pool used by PostgresSaver — TRUE (lines 283-299, same `pool`).
5. With min_size=1, that connection is reused for checkpoint ops — TRUE.

The claim holds up. The only mitigating angle: would the GUC actually be present on the connection re-checked-out? Under `autocommit=True` with `set_config(is_local=false)`, the setting applies at session scope and persists. The advisory unlock and pool rollback do not clear it. So `SHOW lock_timeout` on a re-checked-out connection would indeed return `'30s'`, not `'0'`.

I genuinely cannot disprove the mechanism. Let me do one final check — whether any wrapper or pool subclass adds a reset, or whether get_checkpointer is even reachable in production.

The checkpointer is the public, production-used persistence path. All five links of the causal chain are verified in the actual code, and the only externally-determined fact (psycopg_pool's default check-in behavior) matches the documented psycopg 3.2 pool contract: check-in does a rollback but no `RESET ALL`/`DISCARD ALL`, so a SESSION-scoped GUC survives. No `reset=` callback is configured.

I cannot disprove the finding.

input.reasoning:
I attempted to refute the finding by examining every link in its causal chain in the actual code at src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py and the test harness, and could not break it.

Verified facts in the real code:
1. Line 212: `_acquire_migration_lock` runs `SELECT set_config('lock_timeout', %s, false)` — the literal `false` for is_local means SESSION scope (not transaction-local), so the setting is not bounded to a transaction. TRUE as described.
2. The pool (lines 266-278) is constructed with NO `reset=` callback — only autocommit, prepare_threshold, row_factory, connect_timeout, check, open. A repo-wide grep for `reset`, `DISCARD`, `RESET ALL` finds nothing in the persistence path. So no check-in hook clears the GUC. TRUE.
3. The bootstrap connection is drawn from the very same `pool` (line 283 `with pool.connection() as conn:`) and on block exit is returned to that pool; that same `pool` is then cached (line 298) and handed to `PostgresSaver(pool)` (line 299) for all later checkpoint reads/writes. TRUE — same pool object.
4. Default pool sizing is min_size=1 (line 263), so the single warm connection that ran bootstrap is the one reused for checkpoint ops.
5. The crux external fact — psycopg_pool's default check-in does NOT reset session state — matches the documented psycopg 3.2 ConnectionPool contract: on return it performs a rollback to end any pending transaction but does NOT issue `RESET ALL`/`DISCARD ALL` unless a `reset` callback is configured. A SESSION-scoped GUC set via set_config(is_local=false) is transaction-independent and survives the rollback. (psycopg_pool is the optional `persistence` extra and is not installed in this environment, so this is reasoned from the library contract, not executed — but it is the well-established documented behavior.)

The advisory unlock (line 294) and the implicit pool rollback do not touch lock_timeout. Therefore a connection re-checked-out from the pool after bootstrap will indeed report lock_timeout='30s' (default ASSAY_MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS=30000), not '0', and ordinary checkpoint writes inherit it.

The only weak point is the PoC's impact scenario requiring two runs to contend on the SAME checkpoint row for >30s (distinct thread_ids write distinct rows and would not contend), which bears on severity/likelihood. But the defect itself — a bootstrap-only session GUC silently persisting on the long-lived pooled connection that serves all checkpoint operations — is real and fully confirmed in the actual code. The author's own remediation note (RESET after acquiring, or run bootstrap on a connection that is closed rather than returned to the pool) is the correct fix and is absent. I genuinely cannot disprove the finding.

content.text: Finding C-1 survives. The lock_timeout session-GUC leak is confirmed in the actual code: `set_config('lock_timeout', %s, false)` at line 212 sets SESSION scope, no `reset=` callback exists on the pool, the bootstrap connection is returned to and reused from the same pool that serves all checkpoint operations, and psycopg_pool's default check-in does not clear session GUCs. I could not disprove it.
